chapters.title: A hidden force in human history
chapters.text: What if your vacation could reshape thousands of lives? That is not a superhero plot. It is the real story of Henry L. Stimson. In 1926, he and his wife spent six days in Kyoto, Japan, walking through temples and gardens. The Japanese felt the consequences of that visit almost twenty years later. By then, Stimson was the US Secretary of War. As a civilian executive, he stayed out of most high-level decisions. Yet, when he saw the list of atomic bomb targets, which included Kyoto, Hiroshima, Yokohama, and Kokura, he stepped in to save the city he had visited. It was not easy. The generals wanted the bomb to end the Pacific war, and Kyoto fit the bill as a wartime industrial center and former capital. Still, Stimson persuaded President Harry S. Truman to drop Kyoto from the list. They added Nagasaki instead. Sometimes the most important events are the ones that never happened. Stimson trip to Kyoto rarely makes it into history books, since the coincidence feels unusual. Yet randomness is far more common and important than most people realize. Step into this eye-opening journey through mind-blowing flukes and learn how to make peace with uncertainty.

chapters.title: Flukes and where to find them
chapters.text: Big events should have big causes, right? That is what Brian Klaas thought a few years ago when he went to Zambia to investigate a failed takeover. Was it because citizens did not back the rebels, or was the system too stable for a coup? Klaas wondered. The real reason happened in seconds, when the army commander ran from rebels who tried to kidnap him. Time flukes are everywhere, and our existence as a species depends on them. If that famous asteroid had arrived a minute later, it would not have hit Earth 66 million years ago, dinosaurs would have kept ruling, and humans would never have had a chance to appear. The lottery of the earth is another fluke. Saudi Arabia discovered its oil in 1938, when it was a low-income country built on farming and herding. Today, Saudi Arabia is one of the richest nations on earth. Many factors shape your wins and losses, not just your actions. We do not need to travel far to see flukes in action. One of the most amazing ones sits inside our cells, the mitochondrion. This powerhouse came from the merger of a bacterium and a prokaryotic cell that had no organelles. Over time, the bacterium became a mitochondrion and started supplying cells with energy. Every complex species owes its existence to that merger. A random event does not have to be complex to have a big impact. Weather unpredictability is a clear example. Edward Norton Lorenz showed that tiny changes, like a one-millionth of a degree temperature shift, can shape the weather. That finding gave rise to the butterfly effect, the idea that small local changes, like a butterfly flapping its wings, can produce major effects far away. On August 9, 1945, Kokura weather broke from the forecast of clear skies. Clouds covered the city, so the US pilot gave up on bombing it. Over Nagasaki, the clouds cleared at the last moment, sealing the city fate. The Japanese later coined the phrase Kokura luck for people who narrowly escape disaster. Our eyes evolved from a fluke, a random mutation that created light-sensitive cells.

chapters.title: One person, massive impact
chapters.text: Social factors also shape the fate of humanity. For instance, if Nikita Khrushchev had been ill-tempered, the Cuban Missile Crisis could have ended very differently. Sometimes, even subtler details tip the scales. Most people have heard of Charles Darwin discovery of natural selection, the idea of the survival of the fittest. This idea changed science, but it almost never surfaced because of a quirky belief. As he prepared HMS Beagle for its next voyage, Captain Robert FitzRoy wanted an educated companion to ease his boredom at sea. A friend suggested Charles Darwin, but FitzRoy hesitated. Why? The shape of Darwin nose. The captain believed in physiognomy, the idea that physical features reveal character. FitzRoy thought Darwin nose suggested he would not survive the hardships of the voyage. Luckily, FitzRoy agreed to take the young naturalist aboard, which let Darwin collect the samples that led to his famous discovery. As John Muir wrote, when we try to pick out anything by itself, we find it hitched to everything else in the universe. You do not need a high position or a famous name to make a real impact in an interconnected world. Consider these two stories. In 2001, Elaine Greenberg was heading to meet her colleague Joseph Lott at a conference in New York City. Knowing he loved impressionism, she bought him a tie covered in Monet paintings. She gave it to him just before the conference. Deeply moved, Lott wanted to wear it right away, but Greenberg said, not with this shirt. Even though it would make him late, Lott went back to his hotel to change. That decision saved his life. It was September 11, and the conference was in the World Trade Center. Another story happened to a tourist named Ivan in 2022. While swimming near the Greek coast, he was swept out to sea. After 18 hours of searching, the coast guard gave up hope. Yet Ivan was found alive. He had spotted a soccer ball in the water, held on to it, and survived. When the story made the news, a woman recognized the ball. Her children had lost it while playing by the sea. Treat unplanned events with patience, because you never know when one might save a life.

chapters.title: Is ignoring randomness a wise move?
chapters.text: If flukes keep shaping human history, why do we hardly consider them? A few reasons explain this. First, we are pattern-creating creatures, so the word accidental rarely comes up. In one experiment, American psychologists showed people simple animations of shapes of different sizes moving around the screen at random. Most participants saw it as a story about a big triangle hunting other figures. Biologically, patterns and stories helped us survive. When our ancestors heard a noise, they quickly linked it to a predator. Legends shared around the fire passed wisdom to the next generation. The Moken are a vivid example. On December 26, 2004, the Indian Ocean tsunami struck huge coastal populations and caused heartbreaking damage. Yet every Moken person in Thailand survived. From childhood, they knew the legend of the laboon, giant waves whose approach silenced the cicadas. When they noticed the silence on December 26, the Moken quickly evacuated. Patterns and stories make our lives feel more ordered and certain, which makes flukes seem like oddities. Second, the Enlightenment period strengthened our faith in order. For a long time, researchers acknowledged the importance of both convergence, or regularity, and contingency, or randomness. Yet when Isaac Newton, one of the fathers of the Enlightenment, discovered the laws of gravity and motion, convergence ruled. It seemed like physics could explain everything, leaving no room for randomness. The Enlightenment also introduced reductionism, the idea that we can understand the whole by studying its parts. This works for complicated systems like a Swiss watch, but applying it to complex systems like society, where every part shapes the others, leads to mistakes. Convergence-centered thinking has created long-lasting misconceptions, like the belief that events usually have one cause, that big causes drive big events, and that cause and effect are simple. In fact, events can be random or driven by many causes that mix in different ways. Small changes, like temperature shifts, can decide the fate of entire cities. As Brian Klaas wrote, rather than rewarding intellectual humility, we too often confuse false certainty with confidence and power. If we leave randomness out of the picture, our view of reality gets distorted. In an interconnected world, that can have dire consequences.

chapters.title: Why the world grows more unstable
chapters.text: If the Enlightenment picture of reality is incomplete, what is closer to the truth? Our world is a place where convergence and contingency sit on equal footing, and every action matters. It looks like what Argentine writer Jorge Luis Borges described in The Garden of Forking Paths. When a person does something, the number of paths available to them changes. Reality is even more layered. Our actions, other people actions, natural forces, and many other factors shape the paths that unfold before us. Often, stuff happens is the most honest explanation of what is going on. Today, humanity faces powerful random events more often than in the past. Why? Self-organized criticality describes our situation well. Each event adds to the previous ones, leaving a moderate effect. Then, when a critical point is reached, one change can undermine the whole system and become a high-impact black swan event. If earlier events needed decades to push toward a tipping point, today they need far less time, because of four factors. Globalization means that being closely interconnected raises the risk of global instability. If something big happened in one region in the 1800s, other parts of the world barely felt it. Today, a crisis anywhere influences most of the world. When a ship crashed into a bank of the Suez Canal in 2021, huge amounts of international trade suffered. The internet gives destructive ideas a powerful tool to spread, and prompting destabilizing actions has never been easier. Optimization has pushed systems to their limits, leaving no room for flexibility. A country energy system should be unified for top efficiency, but if something breaks in one region, power cuts ripple across the country. Speed has increased, and many processes unfold much faster than before. Scientists are still working out when an all-changing action occurs, but they have already spotted a red flag, critical slowing down. Stable systems recover from tiny disturbances quickly, but systems approaching instability take far longer to bounce back. The good news is that we can use this knowledge to our advantage.

chapters.title: The upside of uncertainty
chapters.text: Knowing that random events can change our lives dramatically should not make us feel helpless. In a world where coincidences play a decisive role, every action matters. For example, our existence comes from countless flukes across history. If just one ancestor had behaved differently, we would not be here. By the same token, if we were not who we are, many lives would look different. Social media often shows these links, since our posts can reach people in distant corners of the world and improve their lives. Sadly, we often do not know the impact of our actions. But just being aware of our influence is valuable. It keeps us from feeling like a cog in a machine and softens some of the harshest effects of individualism. Self-reliance has many benefits, like pushing us to act rather than only react. But individualism carries two downsides. Loosening our tie to community can weaken what gives life meaning, while seeing ourselves as part of a whole adds meaning. The idea that we are solely responsible for our lives implies we should control what we cannot, and since we cannot, we should stop blaming ourselves when life does not go as we wish. Does accepting randomness mean we should give up? Not at all. We should keep being proactive, but aim for resilience, not efficiency. Building flexibility is one of the most important ways to do that. What does it look like? On a personal level, a checklist lifestyle lets us do more, but it leaves almost no time for experimentation. Yet those activities matter as much as serious tasks. In an uncertain world, it is wise to avoid putting all eggs in one basket and instead keep a few backup plans we discover through exploration. Switching our mind off tasks also gives us room for leisure. On a societal level, instead of funding only the scientists who promise results, authorities and investors should support prominent researchers from many fields, since we never know what topic will matter most tomorrow. A world full of flukes means miracles wait at every corner.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text: Thinking that everything happens for a reason may make us more confident, but it does not match reality. Random events shape our lives every day, whether we want them to or not. So instead of running from uncertainty, we should face it. Reforms in social science can help. If we pay more attention to outliers in data and to fruitless experiments, our view of ourselves will sharpen. I don know should become a valid answer on its own. It is better to pause and explore different paths than to march confidently in the wrong direction. Try this. Schedule 30 minutes a day for exploration. Listen online to The Köln Concert by Keith Jarrett. This jazz classic came from a fluke, since Jarrett had to use all his creativity to hide that the piano was broken. Read The Garden of Forking Paths by Jorge Luis Borges. Read it till the end?
